text: Hill curve fitting key step GeoTox workflow, provides parameters needed link internal concentrations assay responses. fit_hill() function can used fit Hill models dose-response data, resulting parameters can added GeoTox object use subsequent calculations. Sometimes standard deviation estimates may missing. cases, fit_hill() impute missing standard deviations corresponding parameter estimate. desirable, imputed standard deviations can identified using tp.sd.imputed logAC50.sd.imputed columns resulting object’s fit table, fits can removed. resulting Hill curves can visualized plotting fitted curves along original dose-response points.

ℹ 75 more rows # ℹ 6 more variables: logc_max <dbl>, resp_min <dbl>, resp_max <dbl>, #   AIC <dbl>, tp.sd.imputed <lgl>, logAC50.sd.imputed <lgl>  $assay [1] "endp"  $substance [1] "casn" "chnm" hill_params$fit <- hill_params$fit |>   filter(!tp.sd.imputed, !logAC50.sd.imputed) ggplot() +   # Add original dose-response points   geom_point(     data = geo_tox_data$dose_response |>       semi_join(hill_params$fit, by = c("endp", "chnm")),     aes(x = 10^logc, y = resp),     pch = 1,     size = 0.5   ) +   # title: GeoTox Introduction
text: three main steps GeoTox workflow: creating GeoTox object setting various data components, simulating population characteristics exposure, calculating risk scores. Creating GeoTox object requires providing path DuckDB database file. can existing file new file created. several components can set object created. set_boundary(): (optional) Store spatial boundary data BLOB database. boundary data can retrieved later using get_boundary() used visualization. set_simulated_css(): Store pre-simulated steady-state plasma concentration data table database. values used sample_simulated_css() (wrapper function simulate_population()). add_exposure_rate_params(): Add parameters needed simulate exposure rates specific routes, e.g. inhalation. parameters used simulate_exposure_rate() (wrapper function simulate_population()). add_hill_params(): Add parameters Hill curve fitting database. parameters used calc_risk() (wrapper function calc_response()). Population characteristics (age obesity status) exposure can simulated using simulate_population() function, wrapper around simulate_age(), simulate_obesity(), simulate_exposure_rate(), simulate_exposure(). Alternatively, set_sample() can used age /obesity status. resulting simulated data stored database used subsequent calculations. addition, simulate_population() sample steady-state plasma concentrations pre-simulated data using sample_simulated_css(), set_fixed_css() called preparation sensitivity analysis. Risk scores can calculated using calc_response() function, wrapper around calc_internal_dose(), calc_invitro_concentration(), calc_risk(). Sensitivity analysis can performed using sensitivity_analysis().
code: GT <- GeoTox("GeoTox-introduction.duckdb") |>   set_boundary(geo_tox_data$boundaries) |>   set_simulated_css(geo_tox_data$simulated_css) |>   add_exposure_rate_params() |>   add_hill_params(hill_params) GT <- GT |>    simulate_population(     age       = geo_tox_data$age,     obesity   = geo_tox_data$obesity,     exposure  = geo_tox_data$exposure |> mutate(route = "inhalation"),     substance = c("casn", "chnm"),     n         = 150   ) GT <- GT |>    calc_response() |>    sensitivity_analysis()

title: GeoTox Introduction
text: overview GeoTox object can obtained printing . Connecting database allows access various tables store data used workflow. tables exist performing sensitivity analysis example dataset.  several helper functions fetching data database use visualization. Geographical boundary data can retrieved using get_boundary() combined helper functions plotting. Exposure data added database using simulate_population(), calls add_exposure() simulate_exposure(). input data used simulate exposure values stored exposure table can joined substance table retrieve entered chemical information. example plotting normalized mean exposure county chemical name.  Various concentration fields stored concentration table. helper function get_concentration_mean() can used fetch mean values given concentration field, grouped substance, route, location. concentration data can plotted similarly exposure data. examples plotting mean external concentration (C_ext), steady-state plasma concentration (C_ss), internal dose (D_int), vitro concentration (C_invitro) county chemical name.     Several risk metrics stored risk table. helper function get_risk_quantiles() can used fetch quantiles risk metrics grouped assay location. examples plotting median values generalized concentration addition (GCA) independent action (IA) efficacy risk scores hazard quotients using 10% effective concentration (HQ.10).     Results sensitivity analyses stored risk_sensitivity_* tables. helper function get_risk_sensitivity() wrapper around get_risk_values(), can used fetch sensitivity data given risk metric, obtain values varying parameters grouped location specific assay. example plotting sensitivity data IA efficacy risk score using ridgeline density plots.
code: GT GeoTox object Database info:   dbdir: GeoTox-introduction.duckdb Reset seed: FALSE Assays: 13 Substances: 22 Locations: 100 Population: 150 Concentrations: C_ext, C_ss, D_int, C_invitro Risk: GCA.Eff, IA.Eff, GCA.HQ.10, IA.HQ.10 Sensitivity: C_ext, age, css_params, fit_params, weight con <- get_con(GT) DBI::dbListTables(con) [1] "age"                         "assay"  [3] "boundary"                    "concentration"  [5] "exposure"                    "exposure_rate"  [7] "exposure_rate_params"        "fixed_css"  [9] "hill_params"                 "location" [11] "obesity"                     "par" [13] "risk"                        "risk_sensitivity_C_ext" [15] "risk_sensitivity_age"        "risk_sensitivity_css_params" [17] "risk_sensitivity_fit_params" "risk_sensitivity_weight" [19] "route"                       "sample" [21] "simulated_css"               "substance" DBI::dbDisconnect(con) plot_exposure <- function(GT) {   con <- get_con(GT)   withr::defer(DBI::dbDisconnect(con))    boundary <- GT |> get_boundary() |> deframe()    df <- tbl(con, "exposure") |>     left_join(tbl(con, "substance"), by = join_by(substance_id == id)) |>     collect() |>     left_join(boundary$county, by = join_by(location_id)) |>     sf::st_as_sf()    ggplot(df) +     geom_sf(aes(fill = norm), color = "grey70") +     facet_wrap(vars(chnm)) +     geom_sf(data = boundary$state, fill = NA, color = "black") +     scale_fill_viridis_c(transform = "sqrt") +     theme(       axis.text = element_text(size = 5),       strip.text = element_text(size = 5)     ) +     ggtitle("Normalized mean exposure") }  plot_exposure(GT) plot_concentration_mean <- function(GT, col, wrap_var = "chnm") {   con <- get_con(GT)   withr::defer(DBI::dbDisconnect(con))    boundary <- GT |> get_boundary() |> deframe()    # Substance data for joining chemical names   substance_df <- tbl(con, "substance") |> collect()    df <- get_concentration_mean(GT, col) |>     mutate(mean = if_else(mean == 0, NA, mean)) |>     left_join(substance_df, by = join_by(substance_id == id)) |>     left_join(boundary$county, by = join_by(location_id)) |>     sf::st_as_sf()    ggplot(df) +     geom_sf(aes(fill = mean), color = "grey70") +     facet_wrap(vars(.data[[wrap_var]])) +     geom_sf(data = boundary$state, fill = NA, color = "black") +     scale_fill_viridis_c(       transform = "log10",       na.value = "grey90"     ) +     theme(       axis.text = element_blank(),       axis.ticks = element_blank(),       panel.background = element_blank(),       panel.grid = element_blank(),       strip.text = element_text(size = 5)     ) +     ggtitle(paste("Mean", col)) }  plot_concentration_mean(GT, "C_ext") The duckplyr package is configured to fall back to dplyr when it encounters an incompatibility. get_con(GT)   withr::defer(DBI::dbDisconnect(con))    boundary <- GT |> get_boundary() |> deframe()    # Assay data for joining assay names   assay_df <- tbl(con, "assay") |> collect()    df <- get_risk_quantiles(GT, col, quantiles) |>     left_join(assay_df, by = join_by(assay_id == id)) |>     left_join(boundary$county, by = join_by(location_id)) |>     sf::st_as_sf()    ggplot(df) +     geom_sf(aes(fill = value), color = "grey70") +     facet_wrap(vars(.data[[wrap_var]])) +     geom_sf(data = boundary$state, fill = NA, color = "black") +     scale_fill_viridis_c(       limits = c(0, max(df$value, na.rm = TRUE)),       direction = -1,       option = "A",       transform = "sqrt",       na.value = "grey90"     ) +     theme(       axis.text = element_blank(),       axis.ticks = element_blank(),       panel.background = element_blank(),       panel.grid = element_blank(),       strip.text = element_text(size = 5)     ) +     ggtitle(paste(names(quantiles), col)) }  plot_risk_quantile(GT, "GCA.Eff") plot_risk_quantile(GT, "IA.Eff") plot_risk_quantile(GT, "GCA.HQ.10") plot_risk_quantile(GT, "IA.HQ.10") plot_risk_sensitivity <- function(GT, metric, assay) {   df <- get_risk_sensitivity(GT, metric, assay) |>      rename(all_of(c(       "External Concentration"   = "C_ext",       "Toxicokinetic Parameters" = "css_params",       "Weight"                   = "weight",       "Age"                      = "age",       "Concentration-Response"   = "fit_params",       "Baseline"                 = "baseline"     )))   df <- df |>     pivot_longer(cols = everything()) |>     mutate(name = factor(name, levels = names(df)))    idx <- is.na(df$value)   if (any(idx)) {     warning(       "Removed ", sum(idx), " NA from risk sensitivity data.", call. = FALSE     )     df <- df |> filter(!idx)   }    if (nrow(df) == 0) {     stop("No risk sensitivity data to plot.", call. = FALSE)   }    ggplot(df) +     ggridges::stat_density_ridges(       aes(x = value, y = 0, color = name),       calc_ecdf = TRUE,       quantiles = 4,       quantile_lines = FALSE,       fill = NA,       linewidth = 1     ) +     scale_x_log10(guide = "axis_logticks") +     scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +     labs(x = metric, y = "", title = assay, color = 'Varying Parameter') +     theme_minimal() +     theme(       panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),       panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),       axis.text.y = element_blank()     ) }  metric <- "IA.Eff" assay <- c(endp = "TOX21_DT40_LUC") plot_risk_sensitivity(GT, metric, assay) Picking joint text: Load packages create empty list store data.
code: library(dplyr) library(httk) library(httr2) library(purrr) library(readr) library(readxl) library(sf) library(stringr) library(tibble) library(tidyr)  geo_tox_data <- list()

title: GeoTox Package Data
text: Download modeled exposure data AirToxScreen. Results 2020 subset chemicals North Carolina counties included GeoTox package data geo_tox_data$exposure. Note 2020 version census block level data, provided several large files separated region. 2019 version census tract level data, provided single file much smaller download. exposure data labeled chemical names, names can vary across resources reliable way connect datasets using Chemical Abstracts Service Registry Number (CAS-RN) identifiers. Use batch search feature CompTox Dashboard retrieve CAS-RN PREFERRED_NAME chemicals exposure dataset. Use Integrated Chemical Environment (ICE) curated high-throughput screening (cHTS) data identify active chemicals given set assays. Note following can used get list available assays given chemical set. Use ICE API retrieve dose-response data selected assays chemicals, included GeoTox package data geo_tox_data$dose_response.
code: filename <- "Region4b_2020ATS_Exposure_Concentrations.xlsx" tmp <- tempfile(filename) download.file(   paste0(     "https://gaftp.epa.gov/rtrmodeling_public/AirToxScreen/2020/",     "Exposure%20Concentrations/",     filename   ),   tmp ) exposure <- read_xlsx(tmp)  # Normalization function min_max_norm = function(x) {   min_x <- min(x, na.rm = TRUE)   max_x <- max(x, na.rm = TRUE)   if (min_x == max_x) {     rep(0, length(x))   } else {     (x - min_x) / (max_x - min_x)   } }  geo_tox_data$exposure <- exposure |>   # North Carolina counties   filter(State == "NC", !grepl("0$", FIPS)) |>   # Aggregate chemicals by county   summarize(     across(       -c(State:Population), # Note: use "-c(State:Tract)" for 2019 data       list(mean = mean, sd = sd),       .names = "{col}|||{fn}"     ),     .by = FIPS   ) |>   pivot_longer(-FIPS) |>   separate_wider_delim(name, "|||", names = c("chemical", "stat")) |>   pivot_wider(names_from = stat) |>   # Normalize concentrations   mutate(norm = min_max_norm(mean), .by = chemical) # Copy/paste the chemical names into the batch search field # https://comptox.epa.gov/dashboard/batch-search cat(geo_tox_data$exposure |> distinct(chemical) |> pull(), sep = "\n") # Export results with "CAS-RN" identifiers as a csv file, then process in R  # Remove rows without clear chemical matches, investigate manually if desired exposure_casrn <- read_csv("CCD-Batch-Search.csv") |>   filter(DTXSID != "N/A", !grepl("WARNING", FOUND_BY))  # Update exposure data with CompTox Dashboard data geo_tox_data$exposure <- geo_tox_data$exposure |>   inner_join(exposure_casrn, by = join_by(chemical == INPUT)) |>   select(FIPS, casn = CASRN, chnm = PREFERRED_NAME, mean, sd, norm) # Get all supported assays help_text <- request("https://ice.ntp.niehs.nih.gov/api/v1/search/help") |>   req_perform() |>   resp_body_string() supported_assays <- str_split_i(help_text, "Supported assay\\(s\\):", 2) |>   str_split_1("\",\"") |>   str_replace_all(c("\"" = "")) |>   str_trim()  # Search for assays available for given chemids chemids <- unique(geo_tox_data$exposure$casn) resp <- request("https://ice.ntp.niehs.nih.gov/api/v1/search") |>   req_body_json(list(chemids = chemids, assays = supported_assays)) |>   req_perform() result <- resp |> resp_body_json() |> pluck("endPoints") fields <- c("assay", "casrn", "dtxsid", "substanceName", "endpoint", "value") df <- map(fields, \(x) map_chr(result, x)) |>   set_names(fields) |>   bind_cols() available_assays <- df |> distinct(assay) |> pull() |> sort() get_cHTS_hits <- function(assays = NULL, chemids = NULL) {   if (is.null(assays) & is.null(chemids)) {     stop("Must provide at least one of 'assays' or 'chemids'")   }    # Format query parameters   req_params <- list()    if (!is.null(assays)) {     if (!is.list(assays)) assays <- as.list(assays)     req_params$assays <- assays   }    if (!is.null(chemids)) {     if (!is.list(chemids)) chemids <- as.list(chemids)     req_params$chemids <- chemids   }    # Query ICE API   resp <- request("https://ice.ntp.niehs.nih.gov/api/v1/search") |>     req_body_json(req_params) |>     req_perform()    if (resp$status_code != 200) {     stop("Failed to retrieve data from ICE API")   }    # Return active chemicals   result <- resp |> resp_body_json() |> pluck("endPoints")    fields <- c("assay", "casrn", "dtxsid", "substanceName", "endpoint", "value")    map(fields, \(x) map_chr(result, x)) |>     set_names(fields) |>     bind_cols() |>     filter(endpoint == "Call", value == "Active") |>     select(-c(endpoint, value)) |>     distinct() }  assays <- c(   "APR_HepG2_p53Act_1hr",   "APR_HepG2_p53Act_24hr",   "APR_HepG2_p53Act_72hr",   "ATG_p53_CIS",   "TOX21_DT40_LUC",   "TOX21_DT40_100_LUC",   "TOX21_DT40_657_LUC",   "TOX21_ELG1_LUC_Agonist",   "TOX21_H2AX_HTRF_CHO_Agonist_ratio",   "TOX21_p53_BLA_p1_ratio",   "TOX21_p53_BLA_p2_ratio",   "TOX21_p53_BLA_p3_ratio",   "TOX21_p53_BLA_p4_ratio",   "TOX21_p53_BLA_p5_ratio" )  chemids <- unique(geo_tox_data$exposure$casn)  cHTS_hits <- get_cHTS_hits(assays = assays, chemids = chemids) get_ICE_dose_resp <- function(assays = NULL, chemids = NULL) {   if (is.null(assays) & is.null(chemids)) {     stop("Must provide at least one of 'assays' or 'chemids'")   }    # Format query parameters   req_params <- list()    if (!is.null(assays)) {     if (!is.list(assays)) assays <- as.list(assays)     req_params$assays <- assays   }    if (!is.null(chemids)) {     if (!is.list(chemids)) chemids <- as.list(chemids)     req_params$chemids <- chemids   }    # Query ICE API   resp <- request("https://ice.ntp.niehs.nih.gov/api/v1/curves") |>     req_body_json(req_params) |>     req_perform()    if (resp$status_code != 200) {     stop("Failed to retrieve data from ICE API")   }    # Return dose-response data   result <- resp |> resp_body_json() |> pluck("curves")    map(result, function(x) {     tibble(       endp = x[["endpoint"]],       casn = x[["casrn"]],       chnm = x[["substance"]],       call = x[["call"]],       logc = map_dbl(x[["concentrationResponses"]], "concentration") |> log10(),       resp = map_dbl(x[["concentrationResponses"]], "response")     )   }) |>     bind_rows() }  assays <- unique(cHTS_hits$assay)  chemids <- intersect(cHTS_hits$casrn, geo_tox_data$exposure$casn)  dose_response <- get_ICE_dose_resp(assays = assays, chemids = chemids)  # Only keep active calls for assay/chemical combinations geo_tox_data$dose_response <- dose_response |>   filter(call == "Active") |>   select(-call)  # Update dose-response chemical names using CompTox Dashboard data geo_tox_data$dose_response <- geo_tox_data$dose_response |>   inner_join(exposure_casrn, by = join_by(casn == CASRN)) |>   select(endp, casn, chnm = PREFERRED_NAME, logc, resp)

title: GeoTox Package Data
text: Download age data U.S. Census Bureau searching “County Population Characteristics”. subset data North Carolina 2020 included GeoTox package data geo_tox_data$age. Search CDC data “places county data”. Go desired dataset webpage, e.g. 2020 county data, download data selecting Actions → API → Download file. subset data North Carolina included GeoTox package data geo_tox_data$obesity. Use httk package generate C_ss values combinations age group weight status chemical. generation values time-intensive step, one approach generate populations C_ss values initially sample later. simulated C_ss values included GeoTox package data geo_tox_data$simulated_css.
code: # Data for North Carolina url <- paste0(   "https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/popest/datasets/",   "2020-2024/counties/asrh/cc-est2024-alldata-37.csv" ) age <- read_csv(url)  geo_tox_data$age <- age |>   # 7/1/2020 population estimate   filter(YEAR == 2) |>   # Create FIPS   mutate(FIPS = str_c(STATE, COUNTY)) |>   # Keep selected columns   select(FIPS, AGEGRP, TOT_POP) places <- read_csv(   "PLACES__County_Data_(GIS_Friendly_Format),_2020_release.csv" )  # Convert confidence interval to standard deviation extract_SD <- function(x) {   range <- as.numeric(str_split_1(str_sub(x, 2, -2), ","))   diff(range) / 3.92 }  geo_tox_data$obesity <- places |>   # North Carolina Counties   filter(StateAbbr == "NC") |>   # Select obesity data   select(FIPS = CountyFIPS, OBESITY_CrudePrev, OBESITY_Crude95CI) |>   # Change confidence interval to standard deviation   rowwise() |>   mutate(OBESITY_SD = extract_SD(OBESITY_Crude95CI)) |>   ungroup() |>   select(-OBESITY_Crude95CI) set.seed(2345) n_samples <- 500  # Get CASN for which httk simulation is possible. Try using load_dawson2021, # load_sipes2017, or load_pradeep2020 to increase availability. load_sipes2017() casn <- intersect(   unique(geo_tox_data$dose_response$casn),   get_cheminfo(suppress.messages = TRUE) )  # Define population demographics for httk simulation pop_demo <- cross_join(   tibble(age_group = list(     c(0, 2), c(3, 5), c(6, 10), c(11, 15), c(16, 20), c(21, 30), c(31, 40),     c(41, 50), c(51, 60), c(61, 70), c(71, 100)   )),   tibble(weight = c("Normal", "Obese")) )  # Create wrapper function around httk steps simulate_css <- function(   chem.cas,   agelim_years,   weight_category,   samples,   verbose = TRUE ) {   if (verbose) {     cat(       chem.cas,       paste0("(", paste(agelim_years, collapse = ", "), ")"),       weight_category,       "\n"     )   }    httkpop <- list(     method = "vi",     gendernum = NULL,     agelim_years = agelim_years,     agelim_months = NULL,     weight_category = weight_category,     reths = c(       "Mexican American",       "Other Hispanic",       "Non-Hispanic White",       "Non-Hispanic Black",       "Other"     )   )    css <- try(     suppressWarnings({       mcs <- create_mc_samples(         chem.cas = chem.cas,         samples = samples,         httkpop.generate.arg.list = httkpop,         suppress.messages = TRUE       )        calc_analytic_css(         chem.cas = chem.cas,         parameters = mcs,         model = "3compartmentss",         suppress.messages = TRUE       )     }),     silent = TRUE   )    # Return   if (is(css, "try-error")) {     warning("simulate_css() failed to generate data for CASN ", chem.cas)     list(NA)   } else {     list(css)   } }  # Simulate C_ss values simulated_css <- map(casn, function(chem.cas) {   pop_demo |>     rowwise() |>     mutate(       css = simulate_css(.env$chem.cas, age_group, weight, .env$n_samples)     ) |>     ungroup() }) simulated_css <- setNames(simulated_css, casn)  # Remove CASN that failed simulate_css casn_keep <- map_lgl(simulated_css, function(df) {   !(length(df$css[[1]]) == 1 && is.na(df$css[[1]])) }) simulated_css <- simulated_css[casn_keep]  geo_tox_data$simulated_css <- simulated_css |>   map(\(x) {     x |>       mutate(         age_lb = map_int(age_group, first),         age_ub = map_int(age_group, last)       ) |>       select(age_lb, age_ub, weight, css) |>       unnest(css)   }) |>   enframe(name = "casn") |>   unnest(value)

title: Calculate risk sensitivity to a single variable — calc_sensitivity
text: sensitivity analysis makes use C\(_{ss}\) values stored 'fixed_css' table GeoTox database. values determined using pre-simulated C\(_{ss}\) values supplied set_simulated_css() can set using set_fixed_css() prior running function. step automatically done using simulate_population() sample_css = TRUE. five options vary argument: age C\(_{ss}\) values 'age' column 'fixed_css' table used. cases, exposure rates re-simulated using median age location 'sample' table calling simulate_exposure_rate() sensitivity = TRUE. weight C\(_{ss}\) values 'weight' column 'fixed_css' table used. css_params C\(_{ss}\) values 'params' column 'fixed_css' table used. fit_params C\(_{ss}\) values '' column 'fixed_css' table used. cases, standard deviation dose-response model fit parameters set zero calling calc_risk() fixed = TRUE. C_ext C\(_{ss}\) values '' column 'fixed_css' table used. cases, external concentrations re-simulated standard deviations set zero calling simulate_exposure() sensitivity = TRUE. cases , resulting risk table named 'risk_sensitivity_~vary~' (e.g., 'risk_sensitivity_age') GeoTox database. Inputs rate_extra_cols, expos_mean, expos_sd need specified already provided previous call set GeoTox parameters (GT$par).

title: Get risk metric results — get_assay_table
text: Normally 'assay' table created adding Hill model concentration-response fit parameters add_hill_params(). assay input specified fit_hill(), 'assay' table created. get_risk_values(), assay parameter can used filter results based assay details stored 'assay' table. useful multiple assays available database. assay input named vector specifying column name value filter , e.g. assay = c(endp = "mortality"). get_risk_quantiles(), assay data GeoTox database, output "assay_id" column filled NA values. assay data, use get_assay_table() retrieve assay information link "assay_id" assay details. Use table_name parameter specify risk table query. can several risk tables GeoTox database. default table named "risk" created either calc_risk() wrapper function calc_response(). Sensitivity analysis results created either calc_sensitivity() wrapper function sensitivity_analysis() stored tables names like "risk_sensitivity_age", etc. Refer calc_sensitivity() see tables may available. get_risk_sensitivity() wrapper function get_risk_values() around risk tables created original risk computation subsequent sensitivity analysis. column names "baseline" original risk table, column names correspond parameter varied sensitivity analysis.

title: Sample from pre-simulated steady-state plasma concentrations — sample_simulated_css
text: C\(_{ss}\) values sampled 'simulated_css' table, must already exist GeoTox database; can created using set_simulated_css(). minimum characteristics used match individuals sets C\(_{ss}\) values age weight category ("Normal" "Obese"). Additional columns (must exist 'simulated_css' 'sample' tables) can specified using css_extra_cols argument. css_exta_cols provided added GeoTox object parameter list, GT$par. addition 'simulated_css' table, 'sample' 'concentration' tables must also exist GeoTox database. 'sample' table must contain characteristics individuals (age, weight category, additional columns specified css_extra_cols). One way create 'sample' table using set_sample(). 'concentration' table typically created using simulate_exposure() populated rows individual substance combination exposure data available. sampled C\(_{ss}\) values stored new "C_ss" column 'concentration' table. substance_order argument can used specify order substances evaluated sampling C\(_{ss}\) values. default evaluate substances order appear 'substance' table. However, different order needed reason (e.g., replication results previous GeoTox implementation), user can provide named list name column 'substance' table use ordering (e.g., "casn") value vector substance identifiers desired order. provided, substance_order added GeoTox object parameter list, GT$par.

'location' 'route' tables must also exist, created adding rate parameters samples. rate_exta_cols provided added GeoTox object parameter list, GT$par. columns must exist 'exposure_rate_params' table used match 'sample' 'exposure_rate_params' tables simulating exposure rates. Typically function called sensitivity set FALSE. case, function create 'exposure_rate' table simulated exposure rates sample exposure route. sensitivity TRUE, exposure rates simulated sensitivity analysis. Typically used directly user, rather called calc_sensitivity(). case, existing 'exposure_rate' table copied 'exposure_rate_sensitivity' table rate values overwritten.

title: Simulate exposure rates — simulate_exposure_rate
code: # Setup required tables # Note: 'gender' is ignored when using the default rate params sample_df <- tibble::tribble(   ~FIPS, ~age, ~weight, ~gender,   10000, 25, "Normal",   "male",   10000, 35,  "Obese",   "male",   20000, 50, "Normal", "female" ) GT <- GeoTox() |>   add_exposure_rate_params() |>   set_sample(sample_df)  # Simulate exposure rates GT |> simulate_exposure_rate()  # Open a connection to GeoTox database con <- get_con(GT)  # Look at created tables  dplyr::tbl(con, "exposure_rate") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   sample_id route_id  rate #>       <int>    <int> <dbl> #> 1         1        1 0.181 #> 2         2        1 0.272 #> 3         3        1 0.260  dplyr::tbl(con, "sample") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>      id location_id   age weight gender #>   <int>       <int> <dbl> <chr>  <chr>  #> 1     1           1    25 Normal male   #> 2     2           1    35 Obese  male   #> 3     3           2    50 Normal female  dplyr::tbl(con, "location") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>      id  FIPS #>   <int> <dbl> #> 1     1 10000 #> 2     2 20000  dplyr::tbl(con, "route") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      id route      #>   <int> <chr>      #> 1     1 inhalation  # Replace exposure rate params with a new table that includes gender params_df <- tibble::tribble(   ~age_lb, ~age_ub, ~gender, ~mean, ~sd,    0, 49,   "male", 10, 1,   50, 99,   "male", 20, 1,    0, 49, "female", 30, 1,   50, 99, "female", 40, 1 ) DBI::dbRemoveTable(con, "exposure_rate_params") GT |> add_exposure_rate_params(params = params_df)  # Overwrite 'rate' values in existing 'exposure_rate' table # Must specify additional column names # Notice how the column names are added to GT$par str(GT$par) #> List of 1 #>  $ reset_seed: logi FALSE GT <- GT |>   simulate_exposure_rate(rate_extra_cols = c("gender"), overwrite = TRUE) str(GT$par) #> List of 2 #>  $ reset_seed     : logi FALSE #>  $ rate_extra_cols: chr "gender"  # Look at updated 'exposure_rate' table dplyr::tbl(con, "exposure_rate") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   sample_id route_id  rate #>       <int>    <int> <dbl> #> 1         1        1  9.03 #> 2         2        1 11.1  #> 3         3        1 39.3   # Clean up example DBI::dbDisconnect(con) file.remove(GT$db_info$dbdir) #> [1] TRUE

title: Simulate obesity values — simulate_obesity
code: simulate_obesity(   GT,   n = 1000,   overwrite = FALSE,   obes_prev = NULL,   obes_sd = NULL )

title: Simulate obesity values — simulate_obesity
text: GT GeoTox object. n Number individuals simulate per location (default 1000). Ignored 'sample' table already exists. overwrite Logical indicating whether overwrite existing 'weight' values 'sample' table (default FALSE). obes_prev Column name obesity prevalence 'obesity' table (default "OBESITY_CrudePrev"). obes_sd Column name obesity standard deviation 'obesity' table (default "OBESITY_SD").

title: Simulate obesity values — simulate_obesity
text: updated GeoTox object, invisibly.

title: Simulate obesity values — simulate_obesity
text: 'obesity' table containing simulation data must already exist GeoTox database, added using add_obesity(). inputs obes_prev obes_sd assigned default values "OBESITY_CrudePrev" "OBESITY_SD", respectively, provided already set GeoTox object's parameters, GT$par. NULL, provided values also saved GT$par.

title: Simulate obesity values — simulate_obesity
code: # Example obesity simulation data obesity_df <- data.frame(   FIPS = c(10000, 20000),   OBESITY_CrudePrev = c(20, 80),   OBESITY_SD = 5 )  # Simulate weight category values GT <- GeoTox() |>   add_obesity(obesity_df) |>   simulate_obesity(n = 5)  # Open a connection to GeoTox database con <- get_con(GT)  # Look at created tables  dplyr::tbl(con, "sample") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 10 × 3 #>       id location_id weight #>    <int>       <int> <chr>  #>  1     1           1 Normal #>  2     2           1 Normal #>  3     3           1 Normal #>  4     4           1 Normal #>  5     5           1 Obese  #>  6     6           2 Obese  #>  7     7           2 Obese  #>  8     8           2 Obese  #>  9     9           2 Normal #> 10    10           2 Obese   dplyr::tbl(con, "location") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>      id  FIPS #>   <int> <dbl> #> 1     1 10000 #> 2     2 20000  # Replace 'obesity' table with different column names names(obesity_df)[2:3] <- c("prev", "sd") DBI::dbRemoveTable(con, "obesity") GT |> add_obesity(obesity_df)  # Overwrite 'weight' category values in existing 'sample' table # Must specify new 'obesity' column names # Notice how the column names are added to GT$par str(GT$par) #> List of 1 #>  $ reset_seed: logi FALSE GT <- GT |>   simulate_obesity(obes_prev = "prev", obes_sd = "sd", overwrite = TRUE) str(GT$par) #> List of 3 #>  $ reset_seed: logi FALSE #>  $ obes_prev : chr "prev" #>  $ obes_sd   : chr "sd"  # Look at updated 'sample' table dplyr::tbl(con, "sample") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 10 × 3 #>       id location_id weight #>    <int>       <int> <chr>  #>  1     1           1 Normal #>  2     2           1 Normal #>  3     3           1 Normal #>  4     4           1 Normal #>  5     5           1 Obese  #>  6     6           2 Obese  #>  7     7           2 Obese  #>  8     8           2 Obese  #>  9     9           2 Obese  #> 10    10           2 Obese   # Clean up example DBI::dbDisconnect(con) file.remove(GT$db_info$dbdir) #> [1] TRUE

what: Simulate population characteristics and exposures — simulate_population
title: Simulate population characteristics and exposures — simulate_population
text: Simulate age, weight category, exposure rates, external exposures. Sample pre-simulated steady-state plasma concentrations.

title: Simulate population characteristics and exposures — simulate_population
code: simulate_population(   GT,   age = NULL,   obesity = NULL,   exposure = NULL,   simulate_rate = TRUE,   sample_css = TRUE,   ... )

title: Simulate population characteristics and exposures — simulate_population
text: GT GeoTox object. age Data frame age data. obesity Data frame obesity data. exposure Data frame exposure data. simulate_rate Logical indicating whether simulate exposure rates. requires exposure rate parameters added using add_exposure_rate_params(). sample_css Logical indicating whether sample steady-state plasma concentrations (C\(_{ss}\)). requires table simulated C\(_{ss}\) values set using set_simulated_css(). addition, set_fixed_css() called sampling prepare C\(_{ss}\) values sensitivity analysis. ... Additional arguments passed wrapped functions (see 'Additional arguments' section 'Details').

title: Simulate population characteristics and exposures — simulate_population
text: updated GeoTox object, invisibly.

title: Simulate population characteristics and exposures — simulate_population
text: wrapper around several functions: add_age() simulate_age() age simulation. add_obesity() simulate_obesity() weight category simulation. add_exposure() simulate_exposure() external exposure concentration simulation (C\(_{ext}\)). simulate_exposure_rate() exposure rate simulation. sample_simulated_css() sampling steady-state plasma concentrations (C\(_{ss}\)). set_fixed_css() prepare C\(_{ss}\) values sensitivity analysis. user can provide data frames age, obesity, exposure data; provided, corresponding add simulate functions called. user can also specify whether simulate exposure rates sample C\(_{ss}\) values using simulate_rate sample_css arguments, respectively. simulate_rate TRUE, exposure rate parameters must added using add_exposure_rate_params(). sample_css TRUE, table simulated C\(_{ss}\) values must already exist using set_simulated_css().

title: Simulate population characteristics and exposures — simulate_population
text: n Number samples simulate (default 1000). Used simulate_age(), simulate_obesity(), simulate_exposure(). Ignored 'sample' table already exists, case existing sample sizes used. location Column name location ID (default "FIPS"). Used add_age(), add_obesity(), add_exposure(). overwrite Logical indicating whether overwrite existing values (default FALSE). Used simulate_age(), simulate_obesity(), simulate_exposure_rate(), simulate_exposure(). substance Column name substance ID (default "casn"). Used add_exposure(). route Column name exposure route (default "route"). Used add_exposure(). rate_extra_cols Additional columns include exposure_rate table. Used simulate_exposure_rate(). obes_prev, obes_sd Column names obesity prevalence standard deviation (default "OBESITY_CrudePrev" "OBESITY_SD", respectively). Used simulate_obesity(). expos_mean, expos_sd Column names exposure concentration mean standard deviation (default "mean" "sd", respectively). Used simulate_exposure(). css_extra_cols Additional columns include simulated_css table. Used sample_simulated_css(). substance_order Named list specifying order substances. Used sample_simulated_css() set_fixed_css().

title: Simulate population characteristics and exposures — simulate_population
code: # Example simulation data  age_df <- data.frame(   FIPS = rep(c(10000, 20000), each = 19),   AGEGRP = rep(0:18, times = 2),   TOT_POP = 0 ) # FIPS 10000, populate age group 40-44 age_df$TOT_POP[c(1, 10)] = 100 # FIPS 20000, populate age groups 50-59 age_df$TOT_POP[c(1, 12, 13) + 19] = c(200, 100, 100)  obesity_df <- data.frame(   FIPS = c(10000, 20000),   OBESITY_CrudePrev = c(20, 80),   OBESITY_SD = 5 )  exposure_df <- tibble::tribble(   ~FIPS, ~casn, ~route, ~mean, ~sd,   10000, "00-00-1", "inhalation", 10, 1,   10000, "00-00-2", "inhalation", 20, 1,   20000, "00-00-1", "inhalation", 30, 1,   20000, "00-00-2", "inhalation", 40, 1 )  # Note: normally the css_df would have many more rows for each combination of # the non-'css' columns to allow for sampling. css_df <- tibble::tribble(   ~casn, ~age_lb, ~age_ub, ~weight, ~css,   "00-00-1",  0, 49, "Normal",  1,   "00-00-1", 50, 99, "Normal",  2,   "00-00-1",  0, 49,  "Obese", 11,   "00-00-1", 50, 99,  "Obese", 12,   "00-00-2",  0, 49, "Normal", 21,   "00-00-2", 50, 99, "Normal", 22,   "00-00-2",  0, 49,  "Obese", 31,   "00-00-2", 50, 99,  "Obese", 32 )  # Simulate population GT <- GeoTox() |>   add_exposure_rate_params() |>   set_simulated_css(css_df) |>   simulate_population(     age = age_df,     obesity = obesity_df,     exposure = exposure_df,     n = 3   )  # Open a connection to GeoTox database con <- get_con(GT)  # Look at created tables  dplyr::tbl(con, "concentration") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 12 × 6 #>       id sample_id substance_id route_id C_ext  C_ss #>    <dbl>     <int>        <int>    <int> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1     1         1            1        1  8.34    11 #>  2     2         1            2        1 18.5     31 #>  3     3         2            1        1  9.28     1 #>  4     4         2            2        1 20.4     21 #>  5     5         3            1        1 10.3      1 #>  6     6         3            2        1 20.9     21 #>  7     7         4            1        1 28.8     12 #>  8     8         4            2        1 40.4     32 #>  9     9         5            1        1 30.5     12 #> 10    10         5            2        1 39.8     32 #> 11    11         6            1        1 28.9     12 #> 12    12         6            2        1 39.5     32  dplyr::tbl(con, "sample") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>      id location_id   age weight #>   <int>       <int> <int> <chr>  #> 1     1           1    41 Obese  #> 2     2           1    41 Normal #> 3     3           1    42 Normal #> 4     4           2    59 Obese  #> 5     5           2    59 Obese  #> 6     6           2    50 Obese   dplyr::tbl(con, "location") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>      id  FIPS #>   <int> <dbl> #> 1     1 10000 #> 2     2 20000  dplyr::tbl(con, "substance") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>      id casn    #>   <int> <chr>   #> 1     1 00-00-1 #> 2     2 00-00-2  dplyr::tbl(con, "route") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      id route      #>   <int> <chr>      #> 1     1 inhalation  # Note: the 'age', 'weight', 'params', and 'other' columns of the # 'fixed_css' table contain the C_ss values for sensitivity analysis. # For example, the 'age' column doesn't contain ages, but C_ss values. dplyr::tbl(con, "fixed_css") |> dplyr::collect() #> # A tibble: 12 × 7 #>       id sample_id substance_id   age weight params other #>    <int>     <int>        <int> <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> #>  1     1         1            1     6   11.5      1    16 #>  2     2         1            2    26   31.5     21    16 #>  3     3         2            1     6    1.5      1    16 #>  4     4         2            2    26   21.5     21    16 #>  5     5         3            1     6    1.5      1    16 #>  6     6         3            2    26   21.5     21    16 #>  7     7         4            1     7   11.5      2    22 #>  8     8         4            2    27   31.5     22    22 #>  9     9         5            1     7   11.5      2    22 #> 10    10         5            2    27   31.5     22    22 #> 11    11         6            1     7   11.5      2    22 #> 12    12         6            2    27   31.5     22    22  # Clean up example DBI::dbDisconnect(con) file.remove(GT$db_info$dbdir) #> [1] TRUE

title: GeoTox 1.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-19 Reimplementation data now stored DuckDB database rather -memory R object. Majority functions redone new storage structure. Hill curve fits using fit_hill() now bidirectional. Previously positive direction fit.

title: GeoTox 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-01-16 compute_sensitivity() wrapper sensitivity_analysis() now use fixed Css data column orders explicitly set match functions. column orders now enforced underlying functions get_fixed_age(), get_fixed_obesity(), get_fixed_params(). Added set_population(), allows age /obesity status set directly require simulation. simulate_* functions can now handle population sizes vary across regions.

title: GeoTox 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2024-11-15 Initial CRAN submission.
